lesson.intro: Every conversation starts with a greeting. Learn to say hello, goodbye, and ask how someone is.
tips: hola = hello, and it works any time of day.
buenos días = good morning, buenas tardes = good afternoon, buenas noches = good evening/night.
¿Cómo estás? = How are you? Answer with bien (well) or muy bien (very well).

lesson.intro: A few polite words go a long way. Learn please, thank you, and how to excuse yourself.
tips: por favor = please, gracias = thank you.
de nada = you're welcome (literally 'of nothing').
perdón / lo siento = excuse me / I'm sorry.

lesson.intro: Meet the people. Learn the subject pronouns and words for man, woman, boy, girl and friend.
tips: yo = I, tú = you, él = he, ella = she.
Nouns have gender: el hombre (the man), la mujer (the woman).
un amigo = a (male) friend, una amiga = a (female) friend.

lesson.intro: Count from one to ten. Numbers show up everywhere — prices, ages, phone numbers.
tips: uno, dos, tres, cuatro, cinco (1–5).
seis, siete, ocho, nueve, diez (6–10).
uno becomes un before a masculine noun: un gato.

lesson.intro: Name the colors. Colors are adjectives, so they often change to match the noun.
tips: rojo (red), azul (blue), verde (green), amarillo (yellow).
negro (black), blanco (white).
Adjectives usually come after the noun: la casa roja (the red house).

lesson.intro: Learn the days of the week and words like today and tomorrow.
tips: lunes, martes, miércoles, jueves, viernes (Mon–Fri).
sábado, domingo (Sat, Sun). Days are not capitalized in Spanish.
hoy = today, mañana = tomorrow, ayer = yesterday.

lesson.intro: Order and name food and drink. Delicious vocabulary you'll use every day.
tips: el pan (bread), el agua (water), la leche (milk), el café (coffee).
la manzana (apple), el arroz (rice), la carne (meat), el queso (cheese).
Quiero... = I want..., por favor to be polite.

lesson.intro: Introduce your family — mother, father, brothers, sisters and children.
tips: la madre (mother), el padre (father).
el hermano (brother), la hermana (sister).
el hijo (son), la hija (daughter), la familia (family).

lesson.intro: Meet the animals — pets and farm animals alike.
tips: el perro (dog), el gato (cat), el pájaro (bird), el pez (fish).
el caballo (horse), la vaca (cow).
Plurals add -s or -es: los perros, los peces.

lesson.intro: Spanish has two verbs for 'to be'. Use ser for identity and estar for states and locations.
tips: ser: soy (I am), eres (you are), es (he/she/it is) — for who or what something is.
estar: estoy, estás, está — for how or where something is.
Soy Ana. (I am Ana.) — Estoy bien. (I am well.)

lesson.intro: The verb tener means 'to have' — and Spanish uses it for age and feelings too.
tips: tengo (I have), tienes (you have), tiene (he/she has).
Tengo un perro. = I have a dog.
For age, use tener: Tengo diez años. = I am ten years old.

lesson.intro: Put it together with everyday verbs: to eat, to drink, to speak, to want and to live.
tips: comer (to eat), beber (to drink), hablar (to speak), vivir (to live), querer (to want).
Present tense -o for 'I': como, bebo, hablo, vivo, quiero.
Hablo español. = I speak Spanish.

lesson.intro: The boss round. A mix of everything you've learned — greetings, numbers, food, family and full sentences. ¡Buena suerte!
tips: Take your time and read each prompt carefully.
Use the 🔊 button whenever you want to hear the Spanish.
Everything here has appeared in an earlier skill.
